name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَمَالِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
What is the matter with the faithless that they scramble toward you, (36)
```

```arabic
عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ عِزِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
from left and right, in groups? (37)
```

```arabic
أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ (٣٨)
```

```english
Does each man among them hope to enter the garden of bliss? (38)
```

```arabic
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
Never! Indeed, We created them from what they know. (39)
```

```arabic
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ وَٱلْمَغَـٰرِبِ إِنَّا لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
So I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests that We are able (40)
```

```arabic
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٤١)
```

```english
to replace them with [others] better than them and We are not to be outmaneuvered. (41)
```

```arabic
فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
So leave them to gossip and play till they encounter the day they are promised: (42)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ سِرَاعًا كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
the day when they emerge from graves, hastening, as if racing toward a target, (43)
```

```arabic
خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
with a humbled look [in their eyes], overcast by abasement. That is the day they had been promised. (44)
```

## (Commentary)

In the first section of the verses of this *Surah*, following the mention of their request for the punishment, the Glorified mentioned that they shall face a befalling punishment that none can avert. This is the blazing Fire, which drags away the scalp, calling those who turned their backs and retreated, and who amassed wealth and hoarded it.

Then, in the second section, He explained the underlying cause of their affliction with this wretchedness: that man is equipped with the instinct of impatience (الهلع) — *the innate tendency toward anxiety and greed* — and the love of his own well-being. Following one's whims (اتباع الهوى) in the exercise of this instinct leads to arrogance against every truth one encounters, thereby casting him into the eternal Fire. None shall escape this except those who are righteous in deed, who confirm the Day of Judgment, and who are apprehensive of the punishment of their Lord.

In this section of verses—which is the third section—He turns back toward those disbelievers (الكفار) as if in wonder at their state, as they gather around the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hastening toward him from the right and from the left in separate groups, fixing their gaze upon him and never leaving him. He addressed him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: What is the matter with them that they surround you, hastening toward you and clinging to you? Does every man among them desire to enter a Garden of Bliss while he is a disbeliever, when Allah, the Glorified, has already decreed that He shall not honor with His Garden anyone except those believers whom He has excepted? Do they intend to outstrip Allah and frustrate Him by nullifying what He has judged and voiding what He has decreed? By no means! Indeed, Allah, who created them from a lowly sperm-drop, is capable of replacing them with those better than them, and of creating from that which He created them, others who will worship Him and enter His Garden.

Then He commanded the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to cut off their disputation and leave them to indulge and play until they meet their Day which they are promised.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَما لِالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ عِزِينَ** **"What is the matter with the faithless, that they gallop toward you, (36) from the right and the left, in groups?"** (70:36-37). Al-Tabarsi said in *al-Majma'*: "Al-Zajjaj said: The 'muhti'' (المهطع) is the one who fixes his gaze upon something without averting it, and this is characteristic of the enemy's gaze. Abu Ubaydah said: 'al-Ihta'' (الإهطاع) means to hasten. 'Izin' (عزين) refers to groups in separation; the singular is 'izzah' (عزة)." End quote. The word 'qibal' (قبل) with a *kasrah* then a *fathah* means the direction adjacent to something. The 'fa' (ف) in 'fama' (فما) is elided (فصيحة) — *indicating a conditional meaning*.

The meaning is: If the destiny of man, due to his disbelief and arrogance against the truth, is the Fire—except for those believers who were excepted—then what is the matter with those who disbelieve that they are in your presence, fixing their gaze upon you and not lifting their eyes from you, while they are scattered groups to your right and your left? Do they hope to enter the Garden, thereby frustrating Allah and outstripping Him in what He has decreed—that none shall enter the Garden except the righteous among the believers?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ** **"Does every one of them hope to be admitted into a garden of bliss?"** (70:38). The interrogation is for denunciation (الإنكار). That is: What is it that drives them to surround you and hasten toward you? Does the hope of each of them to enter a Garden of Bliss drive them to this, while he is a disbeliever? For a disbeliever has no hope of entering the Garden.

The hope (الطمع) is attributed to "every man" (كل امرئ) among them rather than to their collective group—by saying "do they hope to enter..."—just as the hastening (الإهطاع) was attributed to their collective group by saying "muhti'in" (مهطعين). This is because the hope for happiness and success that is beneficial is the hope residing within the individual's soul, which motivates him toward faith and righteous deeds, rather than that which resides in the group qua group. The hope of the collective, as a collective, does not suffice for the happiness of each individual.

In His saying: **أَنْ يُدْخَلَ** "to be admitted," using the passive voice (مجهولا) from the form of *if'al* (الإفعال), there is an indication that their entry into the Garden is not contingent upon their own choice or will. Rather, if it were to happen, it would be up to Allah, the Glorified; He is the One who admits them into the Garden if He wills, and He will never admit them given His decree that no disbeliever shall enter it.

It has been said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to pray near the Kaaba and recite the Quran, and the polytheists would gather around him in circles and groups, listening and mocking his words, saying: "If these people enter the Garden as Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله says, then we shall surely enter it before them." Thus, the verses were revealed.

However, this view does not align with the context (سياق) of the verses, which clearly shows that their action is a consequence of what preceded regarding the deprivation of people from entering the Garden except for the excepted believers. For it is necessary, based on this view, that their gathering around him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and their hastening toward him was driven only by their excessive enmity and their exaggeration in harming and insulting him. Their saying, "We shall enter the Garden before the believers"—while they were polytheists persisting in the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد) and not acknowledging either Fire or Garden—would have been merely mockery and sarcasm (تهكما).

Thus, there would be no grounds for making their action a consequence of the preceding discourse on the Fire, the Garden, and the question—in a context of wonder—regarding the reason driving them to it, followed by the interrogation and denunciation of their hope to enter the Garden.

Therefore, what was mentioned previously supports the view that the "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا) in His saying: **فَما لِالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** refers to a group of hypocrites (المنافقين) who believed in him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله outwardly and accompanied him, but then disbelieved by rejecting some of what was revealed to him. This is indicated by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا فَطُبِعَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ** **"That is because they believed and then disbelieved, so their hearts were sealed"** (63:3), and His saying: **لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ** **"Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your faith"** (9:66), and His saying: **فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ** **"So He caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts"** (9:77).

These were a people who had believed and entered the community of believers, accompanying the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hastening toward him from the right and the left in groups. Then they disbelieved in some of what was revealed to him, showing no concern for it. So Allah, the Glorified, rebuked them in these verses, stating that they do not benefit from accompanying him, nor do they have any right to hope for entry into the Garden; they are not among those who shall enter it, nor can they outstrip or frustrate Him.

This is supported by the subsequent verse: **إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ** "Indeed We are able to replace them with those better than them," as we shall point out.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** **"No indeed! We created them from what they know."** (70:39). This is a deterrent (ردع) against their hope of entering the Garden while in a state of disbelief.

In His saying: **إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** "We created them from what they know," what they know refers to the sperm-drop (النطفة), for man is created from it.

The speech is linked to what follows, and the whole serves as a justification (تعليل) for the deterrent. The gist of the justification is: We created them from a sperm-drop—and they know this—so We have the power to take them away and create in their place another people who are better than them, who are believers and do not reject anything of the religion of Allah. We are not outstripped such that these disbelievers could frustrate Us and outstrip Us, forcing Us to admit them into the Garden, which would nullify Our decree that no disbeliever shall enter the Garden.

It has been said: The "min" (من) in His saying: **مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** "from what they know" conveys the meaning of the "lam" of justification (لام التعليل). The meaning would be: We created them for the sake of what they know—which is perfection through faith and obedience. Thus, it is mandatory that they clothe themselves with these so that We may admit them into the Garden. How then can they hope to enter it while they are disbelievers? They only came to know this through the report of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

It has also been said: The "min" is for the beginning of an end (ابتداء الغاية). The meaning would be: We created them from a filthy sperm-drop that does not suit the realm of sanctity and purity until it is purified through faith and obedience and takes on the character of the angels to enter [the Garden]. How can they achieve that while they are disbelievers?

It has also been said: The "ma" (ما) in **مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** refers to the genus (الجنس). The meaning would be: We created them from the genus of human beings who know, or from the creation that knows, not from the genus of animals that do not reason or understand. Thus, the proof is established against them and is complete. These three views are weak (سخيفة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ** **"I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests that We are able (40) to replace them with [others] better than them, and We are not to be outmaneuvered."** (70:40-41). What is meant by the easts and the wests (المشارق والمغارب) are the points of sunrise and sunset, for the sun has a different point of rising and setting for every day of the solar year, to which it does not return until the same day of the following year. It is also possible that what is meant are the rising and setting points of all the stars.

In this verse, despite its brevity, there are several instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات). In His saying: **فَلا أُقْسِمُ** "I swear," there is a shift from the first-person plural (التكلم مع الغير) in "We created them" (إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ) to the first-person singular. The reason for this is to emphasize the oath by attributing it to Allah Himself.

In His saying: **بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ** "by the Lord of the easts and the wests," there is a shift from the first-person singular to the third-person (الغيبة). The reason for this is to point to an attribute among His attributes that is the principle (المبدأ) in the creation of people generation after generation, which is His Lordship (ربوبيته) over the easts and the wests. For the rising after rising and setting after setting, which is inseparable from the passage of time, has a complete role in the formation of man generation after generation and all other terrestrial events associated with it.

In His saying: **إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ** "Indeed We are able," there is a shift from the third-person to the first-person plural. The reason for this is to indicate the grandeur (العظمة) appropriate for the mention of power (القدرة). In the mention of His Lordship over the easts and the wests, there is an indication of the justification for this power; for He to whom the governance (تدبير) of events in their formation traces back cannot be frustrated by any of the events—which are His own acts—from achieving any of them. Nor can anything of His creation prevent Him from replacing it with something better than it; otherwise, the preventer would share in the matter of governance, and Allah, the Glorified, is One, having no partner in His Lordship. Understand this well.

In His saying: **إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ** "Indeed We are able to replace them with those better than them," the "ala" (عَلى) is connected to "qadirun" (لَقادِرُونَ). The first object of "nubaddila" (نبدل) is an omitted pronoun referring back to them; it was omitted to indicate the insignificance of their affair and the lack of concern for them. "Khayran" (خَيْراً) is its second object, an adjective standing in place of its noun. The estimation is: "We are able to replace them with a people better than them." Their being better than them lies in their believing in Allah and not disbelieving in Him, and in their following the truth and not rejecting it.

In His saying: **وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ** "and We are not to be outmaneuvered," the meaning of "sabq" (السبق) — *outstripping* — is being overcome, used metaphorically. For Allah to be "outmaneuvered" would mean that His creation of them prevents Him from taking them away and bringing in their stead a people better than them.

The context of the verse is not devoid of some support for what was previously mentioned—that the "those who disbelieve" refers to a group of hypocrites rather than the polytheists who were antagonistic to the religion and denied the very basis of the Resurrection. For the outward meaning of His saying: **خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ** "better than them" is not devoid of an indication or a hint that there is some trace of goodness in them, and Allah may replace them with those better than them. As for the polytheists, there is no good in them; however, this group of hypocrites—their preservation of the outward forms of the religion they believed in and did not reject—is not devoid of some benefit for Islam.

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying: **إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** until the end of the three verses is a justification for the deterrent in His saying: **كَلَّا** "No indeed!" The summary of the content of the three verses is that they are created from a sperm-drop—and they know this—and it is an ongoing creation. Allah, who is the Lord of ongoing events, among which is the creation of man generation after generation, and the Governor of them, is able to take them away and replace them with those better than them who would care for the matter of religion and be worthy of entering the Garden. Their creation does not prevent Him from replacing them with those better than them and admitting them into the Garden due to the perfection of their faith, without being compelled to admit these [hypocrites] into the Garden. Thus, His decree that the Garden is for the righteous among the people of faith is not nullified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ** **"So leave them to indulge and play until they meet their day which they are promised,"** (70:42). This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to leave them and what they are engaged in, not to persist in disputation with them, nor to exhaust himself in admonishing them. What they are engaged in has been called "indulging" (الخوض) and "playing" (اللعب) to indicate that they do not benefit from it in any real sense, despite their deep involvement and persistence in it, just like play which has no benefit beyond the imagination. So let them be left until they meet the Day they are promised, which is the Day of Resurrection.

In the attribution of the Day to them, there is an indication of a specific connection it has with them, which is the connection to their punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ** **"the day they emerge from the graves, hastening as if they were racing toward a target,"** (70:43). This is an explanation of their Day which they are promised, which is the Day of Resurrection.

"Al-ajdath" (الأجداث) is the plural of "jadath" (جدث), which is the grave. "Sira'an" (سراعا) is the plural of "sari'" (سريع) — *fast*. "Nusub" (نصب) is that which is set up as a sign on the road, which travelers head toward to be guided by it. It has been said: It is the idol set up for worship, but this is far-fetched in the speech of the Exalted. "Al-ifad" (الإيفاض) means to hasten. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ** **"with their eyes humbled, overwhelmed by abasement. That is the day they were promised."** (70:44). "Khashu'" (الخشوع) — *humility* — is a specific state of being affected in the heart upon witnessing grandeur and majesty; its counterpart in the limbs is "khudu'" (الخضوع) — *submission*. The attribution of humility to the eyes is due to the appearance of its effects in them. "Al-rahaq" (الرهق) is to be forcefully overwhelmed by something.

His saying: **ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ** "That is the day they were promised" refers to the previously mentioned descriptions: emerging from the graves in haste, the humility of the eyes, and being overwhelmed by abasement.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated that 'Abd ibn Humayd reported from 'Ubadah ibn Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله entered the mosque and said: 'Why do I see you in separate groups (عزين), in circles like the circles of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية)?' A man would sit behind his brother."

**I say:** It was also narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah with the wording: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came out while his companions were sitting in circles and groups, and he said: 'Why do I see you in separate groups (عزين)?'" This meaning was also narrated from Jabir ibn Samurah.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **كَلَّا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ** "No indeed! We created them from what they know," he said: "From a sperm-drop (نطفة), then a clinging mass (علقة)." Regarding His saying: **فَلا أُقْسِمُ** "I swear," he said: "It means 'I swear' **بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَالْمَغارِبِ** 'by the Lord of the easts and the wests'—the easts of winter and the easts of summer, and the wests of winter and the wests of summer."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Abi Hammad, reaching to the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام, who said: "It has three hundred and sixty easts and three hundred and sixty wests; the day it rises in one, it does not return to it until the following year."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً** "the day they emerge from the graves, hastening," he said: "From the grave **كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ** 'as if they were racing toward a target'—they are called toward the caller." Regarding His saying: **تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ** "overwhelmed by abasement," he said: "Abasement afflicts them."
